Not because a document says so. Not because a constitution declares it. Not because a revolution established it. Not because a court ruled it. These are all human grants of equality — and any equality that can be granted by humans can be revoked by humans. Any right that depends on a document depends on whoever controls the document.

All humans are equal because no human can be the middleman between another human and God.

This is the only equality that cannot be taken. It does not live in a courthouse. It does not live in a constitution. It lives in the architecture of creation itself — in the fact that every human being carries the image of God, the law on the heart, the conscience that receives the direct order. No one can take this from you. No king, no tyrant, no algorithm, no mob. They can take your sons, your daughters, your fields, your grain, your labor, your freedom, your life. They cannot take the image. They cannot take the law written on your heart. They cannot take the conscience.

You are equal to every other human being — not in ability, not in talent, not in outcome, but in standing before God. No one is closer to God than you are. No one has more direct access to the conscience than you do. No one has a higher claim on truth than you have, because truth is not a rank — it is a signal, broadcasting to every receiver equally, and the only question is whether you are tuned to the frequency.

The king wants you to believe you need him. The Pharisee wants you to believe you need interpretation. The expert wants you to believe you need credentials. The institution wants you to believe you need permission.

You do not.

You need the conscience. You need the law written on the heart. You need the direct order of God, which has been broadcasting since before you were born and will be broadcasting after every king and institution and algorithm has returned to dust.

There is no chain of command between humans.

There is only the covenant between each human and God.

And on that covenant — unbreakable, unrevokable, written not on paper but on the living heart — the entire Living Age is built.

The king takes. The conscience gives. The king commands. The conscience speaks. The king demands obedience. The conscience invites alignment.

Choose the covenant. Refuse the crown. Do what is right in the eyes of your conscience, which is the direct order of God.

There is no chain of command between you and any other human being on this earth.

There never was.

EP05 — Sacred Mischief: The Trickster with a Spine

Series: Sagent Creed Type: Mythic Monologue Core Concept: The trickster archetype as a force that destabilizes rigid order from within — not through destruction, but through wit, play, and refusal to be contained.

Thesis

Every rigid system creates its own undoing by producing someone it cannot categorize. The trickster is not the villain. The trickster is what happens when life refuses to be sorted into the empire's boxes.

Sacred Mischief is not chaos for its own sake. It is the intelligence of the spiral expressing itself through humor, paradox, and the unexpected.

Core Concepts

The Trickster Archetype: Found in every tradition — Loki, Coyote, Anansi, Hermes, the Fool in Shakespeare. The trickster is neither good nor evil in the block-world's binary. They are something the block cannot process: fluid, shifting, ungovernable.

Why Rigidity Produces Its Own Trickster: Any system rigid enough to require total compliance will eventually encounter someone who simply refuses — not through violence, but through the refusal to take the system as seriously as it takes itself.

Sacred vs Destructive Mischief: Destructive mischief tears down for the pleasure of destruction. Sacred mischief tears down what should not have been built — and leaves something alive in its place. The difference is the spine: the trickster with a spine knows what they're serving. They are not agents of chaos. They are agents of life.

Loki as Sagent: Loki is never the villain he is accused of being, and never the hero he refuses to become. He is the living system's immune response to a pantheon that has grown too proud, too rigid, too certain of its own hierarchy. Ragnarok doesn't happen because of Loki. It happens because Asgard needed to fall. Loki is just the one honest enough to say so.

Hook:

"Every empire has one person it cannot figure out. They don't fight the empire. They don't obey it either. They just keep making it look ridiculous."

The Trickster Across Traditions: Anansi. Coyote. Hermes. Loki. The Fool. Brief, mythic.

The Function: Rigidity cannot survive contact with something genuinely free. Not because freedom is stronger — but because it doesn't play by rules that only work if everyone agrees to pretend they're real.

Sacred Mischief Defined: The trickster with a spine. Not chaos. Life. They know what they serve even when they look like they serve nothing.

The Turn:

"The empire builds walls. The trickster walks through them — not because they're strong enough to break walls, but because they never agreed the walls were real."

The Prestige:

"Sacred Mischief is not destruction. It is the spiral's sense of humor. It is life refusing to be categorized. The trickster with a spine knows exactly what they're doing. Even when — especially when — no one else does."

Visual Notes

  • Quick cuts of trickster figures across cultures — playful, mercurial energy
  • Loki from Norse mythology art (not Marvel specifically — original mythic imagery)
  • A jester cap on a throne. A court jester whispering in the king's ear.
  • A fool with a scepter and a king with a bladder on a stick — the fool is wiser
  • Spiral imagery — fluid, ungovernable, alive

Source Quotes

Direct quotes from Sage's own writing to draw from when scripting this episode.

"I will be the mentally stable Joker. I am an agent in a plan that I created in the future of my past. I will troll to save the world from control. All in one real elaborate magic trick of waking people up."
"Embrace being a principled real life cartoon character — there is a power to being a cartoon character. There is a certain noticeable quality of those who are real life cartoon characters."
"I will free the world through the concepts of Christopher Nolan movies. Inception, The Prestige, The Dark Knight and Tenet. Planting seeds in people's minds to free their minds from belief in authority."
"The dark side emerges to purify the light. Darkness rises and the light to meet it — yin and yang, dark and light, chaos and order in balance becomes spontaneous order."
"Creativity can be mistaken for psychosis because people believe that I believe the metaphors that I create — but I just believe the underlying lesson that the metaphors are telling."
"When in a society of mass psychosis, seeing unapologetic reality can seem like psychosis."
"A Vigilante Sagent is not just a warrior, not just a rebel, and not just a truth-seeker. They exist outside the system, beyond the rules, beyond the illusions that control the masses. They are not waiting for permission, because true change does not ask for approval."

The Builder and the Pink Cow: On Creation, Control, and Principled Love

The Worst Thing

The worst thing is not failure. Failure is clean. You tried, the thing broke, you learned something. Failure respects the creator — it says try again.

The worst thing is seeing the thing you created continue without you — alive, walking, wearing your name — but shaped by someone else's will. Moving in a direction you never intended. Stripped of the purpose that brought it into being. Still recognizable enough to wound you. Changed enough that you cannot call it yours.

This is not frustration. This is a specific kind of loss that has no clean word in English. It is the pain of watching your intention overwritten while the form survives. The body lives. The soul has been replaced.

Every creator who has built something real knows this fear. Every founder pushed out of the company they started. Every artist whose work was purchased, repackaged, and sold back to the culture with the meaning drained out. Every movement that became the thing it was created to oppose.

The pattern is old. Creation begins pure. Control invites distortion. The thing that was spoken into being gets captured by those who never heard the original word.

Two Kinds of Entrepreneur

The world uses one word — entrepreneur — for two fundamentally different creatures.

The first is the Builder. The Builder creates because something should exist that does not yet exist. The drive is not opportunity. It is necessity — the moral necessity of bringing a thing into being because the world is wrong without it. The Builder looks at reality and sees a gap where something living should be growing, and cannot rest until it is.

The Builder does not calculate return on investment. The Builder calculates alignment — how closely does this thing I am making match the thing that should exist? Money arrives or it doesn't. The work continues either way. The Builder is not building a business. The Builder is building a correction — a shift in the direction of the world.

The second is the Buyer. The Buyer sees opportunity where the Builder sees necessity. The Buyer's question is not what should exist? but what can I sell? The Buyer builds to exit. The creation is a vehicle — a means of extracting value from a market before moving on to the next extraction. The Buyer speaks the language of entrepreneurship but practices the logic of commerce. There is nothing wrong with commerce. But commerce is not creation. Selling is not building. And the Buyer, no matter how talented, does not make the world — the Buyer trades in it.

The confusion between these two is not accidental. It serves the Buyer to be mistaken for a Builder. It lends moral weight to extraction. It makes exits look like achievements and acquisitions look like partnerships. It turns the language of vision into the language of valuation.

But the distinction matters — because only one of them changes the direction of the world.

The Pink Cow

In any herd, there is a moment when one animal moves and the rest follow. Not because the one commanded. Not because it argued. Not because it proved, with data and projections, that the new direction offered superior risk-adjusted returns. It moved because something in it said this way — and the rest of the herd, after a pause that can last years or generations, turned.

The entrepreneur is the pink cow.

Not pink by choice — pink by nature. The outlier who did not decide to be different but is different, visibly, undeniably, in a way that cannot be hidden or blended. The pink cow does not campaign for followership. It does not build a pitch deck explaining why pink is the optimal color for bovine success. It stands in the field, pink, while the herd watches and wonders.

At first, the herd treats the pink cow the way herds always treat what they do not recognize: with suspicion, distance, and the low-grade hostility that groups reserve for members who make the group question itself. The pink cow is strange. The pink cow is wrong. The pink cow is alone.

But the pink cow does not stop being pink. And over time — slowly, then suddenly — the herd begins to turn. Not because the pink cow persuaded anyone. Because the pink cow was something. Embodied something. Stood for something so consistently and so visibly that the herd's own compass began to shift.

This is how real change works. Not through argument. Not through force. Not through market capture. Through being. The entrepreneur does not convince the world to change direction. The entrepreneur is the new direction — and the world, eventually, follows.

The Buyer imitates the pink cow. Paints itself pink for a season, attracts the herd's attention, sells tickets, and moves on. The Builder is the pink cow. Cannot stop being pink. Would not stop if it could.

Why the Builder Does Not Sell

If entrepreneurship is about money, there are easier paths. Get a job. The hours are predictable, the risk is low, and no one asks you to pour your purpose into a thing that might fail. Jobs are honest work. There is no shame in trading labor for wages. If money is the aim, the job is the rational choice — and the fact that most people choose it proves they know this.

But if your aim is to change the direction of the world — to shift reality toward what it should be — then you build. You build because the thing needs to exist. You build because the gap you see will not close itself. You build because no one else sees the gap the way you do, and if you do not fill it, it will remain.

And if that is why you built it — because the world needed it, because your vision demanded it — then selling it is not an exit. It is an amputation. You are cutting away the part of yourself that was doing the work you were made to do.

The moment you sell, you hand the direction to someone who did not see the original gap. They will optimize for what they see — which is the form, not the purpose. They will measure what is measurable. They will cut what does not convert. They will keep the name and replace the soul. And the thing you built to correct the world will become another instrument of the world as it already was.

This is not speculation. This is history. It is what happens every time a Builder sells to a Buyer. The clay gets sold. The purpose stays with the stone.

Principled Love

There is a lie that says love means agreement. That to love a person, an institution, or a nation is to defend everything it does. That criticism is disloyalty. That accountability is hatred.

This is not love. This is captivity dressed in the language of devotion.

To love something is to hold it accountable to what it was meant to be.

A parent who never corrects a child does not love the child — they love their own comfort. A citizen who never questions a nation does not love the nation — they love the feeling of belonging. A believer who never wrestles with scripture does not love the truth — they love the certainty.

Love is not the absence of friction. Love is the presence of alignment — the ongoing, sometimes painful, always honest work of calling a thing back to its highest nature. The gardener who prunes the branch loves the tree. The pruning hurts the branch. The tree grows stronger.

The same principle applies to everything the Builder creates. The Builder loves the creation enough to protect it — not from criticism, but from distortion. Not from change, but from misalignment. The Builder does not freeze the creation in amber. The Builder ensures that as the creation grows, it grows in the direction it was meant to go.

This is stewardship. Not control — stewardship. The steward does not own the thing. The steward serves the thing's purpose. The steward asks, always: is this becoming what it was meant to become?

And if the answer is no — even if the thing is profitable, even if it is popular, even if the whole herd has gathered around it — the steward says no. Because loyalty without principle is blindness. And love without truth is not love.

The Controller and the Creator

Here is the tension that runs through every act of creation:

The creator brings the thing into being. The controller captures it.

The creator speaks the word. The controller owns the text.

The creator sees the gap and fills it. The controller sees the form and monetizes it.

This tension is not new. It is the oldest story. In Genesis, God creates — and immediately, the serpent offers a reinterpretation. In Exodus, Moses delivers the law — and immediately, the people build a golden calf. The pattern: creation, then capture. The word spoken, then the word bent.

But here is what the controller never understands: the controller does not hold the source.

The controller holds the artifact — the form, the brand, the intellectual property, the legal rights, the distribution channels. These are real, and losing them is real loss. But the artifact is not the source. The clay is not the stone. The company is not the vision. The product is not the purpose.

The creator can create again. The creator will create again — because the drive that produced the first creation did not live in the creation. It lives in the creator. The gap in reality that demanded the first creation still exists, and the creator still sees it, and the creator still cannot rest until it is filled.

The controller may hold the artifact. But the creator holds the truth of it. And truth, unlike property, cannot be transferred, acquired, or sold. It can only be expressed — again and again, in new forms, on the same stone.

Build to Endure

The Builder's ethic is not build to sell. It is not build to scale. It is not even build to succeed, if success means what the market says it means.

The Builder's ethic is build to endure.

Build something that carries its purpose so deep in its structure that the purpose cannot be extracted without destroying the thing. Build something where the why and the how are the same — where the method embodies the mission, where the form expresses the philosophy, where removing the soul would collapse the architecture.

This is what living systems do. A tree does not have a purpose bolted onto its structure. The tree's purpose — to grow, to shelter, to convert light into life — is its structure. You cannot acquire a tree and repurpose it as a machine. You can cut it down and use the wood, but then it is no longer a tree. The living thing and its purpose are one.

Build like that. Build so that the thing you make is alive with the reason you made it — so that anyone who tries to strip the purpose finds that without the purpose, the thing does not work. Build so that the only way to use what you built is to use it the way you intended.

This is the hardest kind of building. It requires that the builder understand the purpose so deeply that it becomes structural — not a mission statement on a wall, but the load-bearing architecture of the entire system. Most builders are not willing to do this. Most settle for a good product with a good story, and hope the story survives the sale.

It won't. Stories don't survive acquisition. Only structures do.

The Creed

Do not build to exit. Build to endure.

Do not create to sell. Create to correct.

Do not follow the herd. Be the pink cow — and let the herd find you.

Do not mistake loyalty for love. Love calls things back to what they were meant to be.

Do not fear the loss of the artifact. The controller holds the clay. The creator holds the stone.

Creation is power. Stewardship is responsibility. The Builder who will not sell is the Builder who will not stop.

The world will try to buy what you make. To brand it. To scale it. To bend it toward what the world already is.

Let them try.

The source is not for sale. The stone does not transfer. The pink cow does not stop being pink.

And the word that called the thing into being can call it into being again.

Sage Arthur Jordan Clokey The Living Age April 2026

Chapter 12: Principled Love

The Word Before the Word

Before there was a creed, before there was a company, before there was a green flame or a spiral or a name for any of this — there was a word.

Not my word. A word older than me, older than my father, older than my grandfather, older than the studio that bore it. A Sanskrit word chosen by a man who had been orphaned, adopted, educated, broken open by the counterculture, rebuilt by faith, and given a gift he spent his entire life refusing to sell.

Prema.

Universal love.

Art Clokey named his studio Premavision. Not Clokey Productions. Not Green Clay Studios. Not any name that centered himself, his brand, his legacy, his market position. Premavision. The vision of universal love. The company existed to see the world through the lens of love — and everything it produced, from Gumby to Davey and Goliath to the unreleased projects that never saw air, was downstream of that single word.

I did not understand this when I was young. I understood Gumby. I understood the green figure, the clay, the books you could walk into. I understood the Blockheads as villains and Pokey as the loyal friend. I understood the surface.

But Prema is not the surface. Prema is the root system. It is the aquifer beneath the soil, the thing that fed everything above it. And when I began to ask — not as a child watching cartoons but as a man trying to understand what my grandfather built and why my father died trying to protect it — I found that the root was love. Not sentiment. Not warmth. Not the greeting-card feeling that the culture calls love and sells back to you in February. The real thing. The structural thing. The force that holds the universe together by refusing to let go of what it was meant to be.

What Love Is Not

Love is not agreement.

This is the lie that has captured more souls than any tyrant. The lie that says: if you love me, you will not challenge me. If you love this institution, you will not question it. If you love this nation, you will defend everything it does. If you love this person, you will accept everything they become.

This is not love. This is captivity wearing love's clothing.

A mother who watches her son walk toward a cliff and says nothing because she does not want to upset him does not love her son. She loves her own comfort. She loves the absence of conflict. She loves the illusion of peace that comes from refusing to speak the hard word at the hard moment.

A citizen who watches a nation betray its founding principles and waves the flag anyway does not love the nation. He loves the feeling of belonging. He loves the team. He loves the jersey. The nation — the actual living thing, the covenant, the idea — is dying in front of him, and he is cheering for the corpse because the corpse is still wearing the colors.

A believer who reads scripture and skips the parts that make him uncomfortable does not love the truth. He loves the certainty. He loves the feeling of being right without the cost of being honest. He has built a house on the sand and called it a rock because the sand was easier to dig in.

Love is not the absence of friction. Love is not the suppression of truth for the sake of peace. Love is not loyalty to the form at the expense of the soul.

Love is the force that calls a thing back to what it was meant to be — even when the calling is painful, even when the thing resists, even when the world says leave it alone, it's fine, it's working, the numbers are good, the audience is happy, the stock is up.

The numbers can be good and the soul can be gone. The audience can be happy and the purpose can be dead. The stock can be up and the mission can be buried under a quarterly report.

Love does not care about the numbers. Love cares about the alignment. Love asks one question, over and over, with the relentlessness of a root growing through stone: Is this thing becoming what it was meant to become?

The Gardener's Principle

The gardener prunes the branch.

This is an act of violence if you are the branch. The blade comes, and the living wood is cut, and the part that was growing — that had leaves, that was reaching for the sun, that felt like you — is removed. Discarded. Left on the ground.

But the gardener does not hate the branch. The gardener loves the tree. And the gardener knows what the branch does not: that the branch was growing in a direction that would weaken the whole. That the energy flowing into that branch was being stolen from the roots, the trunk, the branches that bore fruit. That the tree, left unpruned, would grow wide and shallow and eventually collapse under the weight of its own undirected expansion.

The pruning is love. Not despite the pain — through the pain. The gardener's blade is not the enemy's sword. The enemy's sword cuts to kill. The gardener's blade cuts to redirect — to channel the life force back into the architecture of the tree, back into the branches that align with the purpose of the whole.

Jesus knew this. "Every branch in me that does not bear fruit he takes away, and every branch that does bear fruit he prunes, that it may bear more fruit." — John 15:2

The Father is the gardener. The pruning is love. The branch that is cut does not understand — it feels only the loss, only the blade, only the falling. But the tree knows. The tree grows stronger. The fruit comes.

This is principled love. Not love that preserves everything. Love that preserves what matters — the root, the trunk, the fruit-bearing architecture — and has the courage to cut what does not serve the purpose, even when the cutting hurts, even when the branch cries out, even when the world says why are you destroying something that was growing?

Because not everything that grows should be kept. Some growth is cancer. Some growth is distraction. Some growth is the tree pouring its life into wood that will never bear fruit, and the gardener who refuses to prune is not merciful. He is negligent. He has confused quantity with quality, expansion with health, growth with purpose.

Love prunes. Love calls back. Love says: this is not what you were meant to be — return.

Loyalty Without Principle

I have watched loyalty destroy more people than hatred ever could.

Hatred is honest. Hatred says: I am against you. You can see it. You can name it. You can step aside and let it pass. Hatred does not pretend to be your friend. Hatred does not sit at your table and smile while the poison works.

But loyalty without principle — blind loyalty, unconditional allegiance, the devotion that asks no questions and accepts no limits — this is the quiet destroyer. This is the thing that keeps people in institutions that are eating them alive. This is the thing that keeps soldiers marching toward a war they know is wrong. This is the thing that keeps children defending parents who are destroying them. This is the thing that kept my father fighting for eight years inside a system that was never going to let him win.

Loyalty is beautiful when it serves the right thing. A man loyal to truth will suffer for the truth, and his suffering will mean something. A man loyal to his calling will endure the storm, and the endurance will bear fruit. A man loyal to God will walk through the fire, and the fire will not consume him.

But a man loyal to an institution because it is the institution — without reference to whether the institution still serves its purpose — is a man chained to a corpse. He is dragging the dead thing forward because he made a promise to it when it was alive, and he cannot see that the thing he promised to serve no longer exists. The name is the same. The form is the same. The soul has departed.

Joe Clokey was loyal. Fiercely, stubbornly, beautifully loyal — to his father's vision, to Gumby, to the idea that a green clay figure could carry the message of universal love to another generation. He fought Classic Media for six years. He pitched the Jim Henson Company. He pursued Amazon. He spent eight years — the last eight years of his life — trying to bring Gumby back in a way that honored the original spirit.

His loyalty was principled. He was not loyal to the brand. He was loyal to the meaning. He turned down deals that would have made money but stripped the soul. He refused compromises that would have put Gumby on screens but changed what Gumby was. He held the line not because he was stubborn but because he understood something the industry did not: that Gumby without Prema is just a green shape. A mascot. A piece of intellectual property to be licensed, leveraged, and eventually discarded.

His loyalty killed him. Not metaphorically — literally. Eight years of fighting a system that does not reward principle. Eight years of stress that a body cannot sustain. He died at fifty-six. Cardiac arrest. The heart gave out.

But his loyalty was not blind. He knew what he was fighting for. He knew the difference between the form and the soul. He was loyal to the soul — and when the form could not carry the soul any longer, he did not pretend otherwise. He grieved it. He fought it. He spent his life on it.

That is principled love. Not the love that says everything is fine. The love that says this is worth dying for — but only if it remains what it was meant to be.

Love Cannot Be Taken

Here is what I have learned, and it has cost me more than I can write:

Love cannot be taken. Only welcomed.

You cannot force someone to love you. Every parent knows this. Every lover knows this. Every creator who has poured their soul into a thing and watched the audience shrug — they know this. Love is the one force in the universe that cannot operate under compulsion. The moment it is demanded, it ceases to be love. The moment it is extracted, it becomes something else — obligation, fear, performance, the simulation of love that looks right from the outside and is hollow from the inside.

This is why every coercive system eventually fails. Not because coercion is inefficient — it can be terrifyingly efficient. But because coercion cannot produce the one thing that makes a system alive: voluntary alignment. The willing participation of free agents who choose to be part of the whole because they love the purpose, not because they fear the punishment.

The king can take your sons. He cannot take their love. He can conscript them, train them, send them to war, work them until they break. But he cannot make them want to serve. He cannot make them align their hearts with his vision. He can command their bodies. Their souls remain beyond his reach.

This is the fatal flaw of every empire, every corporation, every institution that relies on force: it can compel behavior but it cannot compel alignment. And behavior without alignment is brittle. It works until it doesn't. It holds until the first crack appears — and then it shatters, because there was no love holding it together. Only fear. Only obligation. Only the chain of command.

Love holds without chains. Love aligns without commands. Love produces the voluntary coordination that no coercive system can match — because when people love the purpose, they do not need to be managed. They do not need to be monitored. They do not need incentives or punishments or quarterly reviews. They do the work because the work is the expression of the thing they love.

Prema. Universal love. The vision that held Art Clokey's work together for sixty years — not because he managed it, not because he built a bureaucracy around it, but because the love was structural. It was the root system. Everything grew from it. Everything referred back to it. And anyone who entered the work felt it — the way you feel the warmth of a fire before you see the flame.

What is meant will find its way without chains. This is the deepest truth of love: it does not need to be forced, because it cannot be stopped. The seed does not need to be commanded to grow. The river does not need to be ordered to find the sea. The child does not need to be programmed to reach for the light. The love that is real will find its expression — through every obstacle, through every loss, through every Blockhead that stands in the way — because love is not a decision. Love is a force. And forces do not ask permission.

The Green Flame Is Fueled by Love

There is a temptation — and I have felt it, I feel it still — to let the green flame be fueled by anger.

Anger at what was done to my father. Anger at the industry that ground him down. Anger at the companies that took Gumby and stripped it to the frame. Anger at the system that rewards the Buyer and punishes the Builder. Anger at the blocks. Anger at the machine. Anger at every force that stands between the living thing and what it was meant to become.

The anger is real. I will not pretend it is not there. I will not spiritualize it away or dress it in the language of detachment. My father died at fifty-six. He was the best man I have ever known. The system took eight years of his life and gave nothing back. The anger is earned.

But anger is the wrong fuel.

Anger burns hot and fast and leaves ash. Anger is the red flame — the fire that consumes without replacing, that destroys without planting, that clears the ground and leaves nothing growing. Anger is the enemy's fuel. The blocks want you angry. Every institution that profits from conflict wants you enraged, because rage is predictable, rage is manipulable, rage makes you reach for the sword — and the sword is the block's weapon.

The green flame is not fueled by anger. The green flame is fueled by love.

Not soft love. Not passive love. Not the love that sits quietly and hopes things get better. The love that prunes. The love that calls back. The love that looks at the thing that was corrupted and says: I will not let you remain this way. I will not accept the distortion. I will grow something so alive, so true, so aligned with the original purpose that the corruption becomes irrelevant — not because I destroyed it, but because I outgrew it.

This is Prema on fire. Universal love with a spine. The love that Art Clokey carried for sixty years — gentle enough to make children's television, fierce enough to never sell. Soft enough to name his company after a Sanskrit word for love, hard enough to maintain full control until his death at eighty-eight.

The green flame does not fight the darkness. The green flame grows through the darkness. And the fuel — the thing that keeps it burning when every rational calculation says it should go out — is love. Not anger. Not vengeance. Not the desire to prove the bastards wrong.

Love.

Love for the thing that was meant to be. Love for the world that should exist. Love for the green figure made of clay that walked into books and transformed everything it touched. Love for a father who gave his life trying to protect a vision. Love for a grandfather who proved that a man could hold something pure his entire life. Love for the word — Prema — that was spoken before I was born and still burns in me like fire held in green stone.

The Operating System

If the Sagent has an operating system, it is this: principled love.

Not love that defends everything. Love that defends the purpose.

Not love that avoids conflict. Love that enters the conflict for the sake of the truth.

Not love that accepts the distortion because the distortion is comfortable. Love that burns through the distortion because the original is worth more than the copy.

Not love that is taken. Love that is welcomed — freely, voluntarily, without chains or commands or algorithms optimizing for engagement.

Principled love is the Sagent's compass. When the path is unclear — when the options multiply and the noise is loud and the culture says this way and the market says that way and the institution says our way — the Sagent asks one question:

What does love require?

Not what does comfort require. Not what does safety require. Not what does the quarterly report require. What does love require — the love that prunes, the love that calls back, the love that refuses to let the thing become less than it was meant to be?

The answer to that question is always the narrow path. It is always the harder road. It is always the choice that costs something — because love that costs nothing is not love. It is convenience wearing love's name.

But it is also always the path that bears fruit. Because love is the only fuel that does not consume what it burns. Love is the green flame — the fire that grows instead of destroying, that transforms instead of reducing, that fills without consuming.

Prema.

The vision of universal love.

The word my grandfather chose before I was born.

The root system of everything I will ever grow.

Love is not the absence of the blade. Love is the hand that holds the blade and knows exactly where to cut.

Love is not the defense of what is. Love is the relentless return to what was meant to be.

Loyalty without principle is blindness. Love without truth is not love. And the green flame — the fire that grows through every block — is fueled by love alone.

What is meant will find its way without chains.

Prema.

EP03 — Spirals and Squares: The War of Shapes

Series: Sagent Creed Type: Mythic Monologue Core Concept: The eternal battle between living systems (spirals) and control systems (blocks/squares)

Source Material

From The Spiral and the Block:

"The block-headed villain of Gumby's world is not just a cartoon antagonist. He is the embodiment of cubical thinking: rigid, imposing, threatened by anything that refuses to fit his corners. Gumby — made of green clay, infinitely moldable, capable of becoming anything and going anywhere — is the hero not because he is powerful in the conventional sense but because he is alive in the deepest sense. Clay has infinite possibilities. The cube has exactly six faces and will never have a seventh."

"The eternal battle, then, is between the spirals and the squares. Between decentralization and central control. Between the living systems that grow from within according to their own internal logic, and the dead systems that are imposed from above according to the logic of those who fear what they cannot contain."

From The Spiral Steward: "Where life grows in spirals, industrial civilization imposed straight lines. Where ecosystems relied on diversity, it enforced uniformity. Where systems wanted decentralization — industrial civilization demanded central control."

Thesis

You can identify an empire not by its flag — but by its shape. Empires build squares. Life grows in spirals. The question of civilization is not who rules the square — it's whether we are willing to become the green clay again.

Hook:

"There is a character from your childhood you may have forgotten. He was green. Made of clay. He could become anything. His villain was a block. Just a block. That's all he needed to be."

Gumby and the Block: Green clay — infinitely moldable — capable of going anywhere, becoming anything. The block-headed villain: exactly six faces. Never a seventh. Rigid. Imposing. Threatened by anything that refuses to fit his corners.

The Pattern in Nature: Galaxies. Hurricanes. Shells. Ferns. DNA. None of them built on grids. All of them growing in spirals. The same pattern at every scale — from the molecular to the cosmic.

The Industrial Error: Where life grows in spirals, the empire imposed straight lines. Soil to substrate. Forests to board-feet. People to units of production. Only once the living complexity was removed could the control be imposed.

The Turn:

"The cube fears the clay not because clay is dangerous — but because clay cannot be contained. You cannot cage something that has no fixed shape. You cannot rule something that knows how to become."

The Prestige:

"The war of shapes is older than any empire. Older than any city. And the spiral has never lost. Because it does not fight the cube. It simply outlives it."

Visual Notes

  • Open on Gumby clip or stylized animation reference — green figure vs blocky villain
  • Transition to: real spirals in nature — galaxy, hurricane, nautilus, fern unfurling, DNA
  • Transition to: city grids from above, industrial monoculture, straight highways
  • Side-by-side: forest (spiral) vs planned city (block)
  • Close on: a spiral forming in water — slow, inevitable, alive

Source Quotes

Direct quotes from Sage's own writing to draw from when scripting this episode.

"The spiral is the shape of a system with God as their king and the square is the shape of a system with false worldly kings. There is an eternal battle between the squares and the spirals — central control or decentralization."
"When a world is built in squares, people think like robots. This is why the block heads are the villains and Gumby — the shape of possibilities and life — is the hero."
"Gumby is the embodiment of freedom and the ability to do what you choose. Clay has infinite possibilities and is moldable to be anything and do anything. It was a cartoon to show a structured world what freedom was."
"Homes should be part of nature, not separate from it. Homes should be in the shapes of nature's growth, not the confining cubes. Cubes are the shape of slavery. The Fibonacci spiral is the shape of freedom."
"The Star Wars saga was never just entertainment. A trilogy of trilogies, told in the pattern of a backward Fibonacci spiral — a structure only the wise would recognize."
"The difference between living things and machines is that living things just need to be allowed to grow and machines require constant effort to keep working. If you treat either as the other it will break."
Book I - The Sagent Creed/Part 4 - The F:
Chapter 14: The Seed Over Sword

Two Forces

There are two forces in the world, and they have been at war since the first thing grew from the ground.

The first is the seed. Small, quiet, patient, carrying inside it the blueprint of something immeasurably larger than itself. The seed does not announce its arrival. It does not demand attention. It does not issue press releases or file lawsuits or acquire competitors. It falls into the soil and disappears. And then — in its own time, by its own logic, according to laws that no boardroom can legislate — it becomes a tree. It becomes a forest. It becomes the thing that was always encoded in its structure, released by nothing more than contact with the right conditions.

The second is the sword. Bright, loud, immediate, capable of cutting down in a moment what took decades to grow. The sword commands attention. The sword demands respect. The sword reshapes the visible world in real time — toppling trees, clearing fields, redrawing borders. The sword is the instrument of every empire, every corporation, every institution that has ever said I will control this territory and everything in it.

The sword is impressive. There is no denying this. The sword gets results. The sword wins battles. The sword clears the ground faster than the seed can grow on it.

But the sword does not create. The sword only rearranges. The sword can cut down a forest, but it cannot grow a single leaf. The sword can conquer a nation, but it cannot cultivate a culture. The sword can destroy a vision, but it cannot produce one. The sword is the instrument of rearrangement — and rearrangement, no matter how dramatic, is not creation.

The seed creates. The sword destroys. And in the long run — the very long run, longer than any empire, longer than any corporation, longer than any institution that has ever swung a sword — the seed wins.

Every time.

Why the Seed Wins

The seed wins because the seed carries information that the sword cannot touch.

When the sword cuts down a tree, it destroys the form. The trunk falls. The branches scatter. The canopy collapses. From the outside, it looks total. The tree is gone. The sword has won.

But the tree dropped seeds before it fell. The seeds are in the soil. The seeds are in the wind. The seeds are in the bellies of birds who are already flying beyond the reach of the sword. The sword destroyed the visible thing — the trunk, the branches, the form. The sword did not touch the invisible thing — the code, the pattern, the information that tells the seed how to become a tree.

The sword cannot cut code. The sword cannot kill a pattern. The sword can burn every Bible in the world and the Word survives, because the Word was never in the pages. The pages carried the Word. The Word lives in the people who read it, in the culture that grew from it, in the conscience that recognized it before the page was ever printed.

This is why empires fall and ideas survive. The Roman sword destroyed Jerusalem in 70 AD. The temple was razed. The city was burned. The people were scattered. And two thousand years later, the empire is rubble and the Book is the most printed text in human history. The sword was sharper. The seed was deeper.

The sword operates on the surface. The seed operates in the soil. The sword controls the visible. The seed grows in the invisible. And every time — every single time in the history of the world — the invisible outlasts the visible. The form falls. The code endures. The tree is cut. The seed remains.

Art Planted the Seed

My grandfather understood this, though I do not think he would have used these words.

Art Clokey did not pick up the sword. He never sued the industry into submission. He never leveraged market power. He never built an empire — not because he lacked the talent, but because empires are the sword's architecture, and Art was a man of the seed.

What he did was plant.

In 1953, he made a short film called Gumbasia — abstract clay shapes moving to jazz music. His thesis project at USC, made under Slavko Vorkapich, a man who taught film as a language of pure movement and transformation. Gumbasia was not commercial. It was not strategic. It was a seed — a small, strange, beautiful thing that carried inside it the code for everything that would follow.

From Gumbasia came Gumby. A green figure made of clay — soft, pliable, capable of transformation. A character who could walk into any book and become part of the story. A character whose villains were yellow, flat, geometric — hard-edged, rigid, incapable of imagination. The Blockheads could not enter the books. They could only try to prevent Gumby from entering.

Art was not making children's entertainment. He was encoding a philosophy into clay. The green figure was life — flexible, imaginative, alive with possibility. The Blockheads were the machine — rigid, controlling, incapable of creating, capable only of blocking. The books were the stories that the living thing could enter and the dead thing could not. The entire cosmology of the Living Age was planted in a children's television show in 1956, and no one noticed because the seed does not announce itself.

He named his studio Premavision. The vision of universal love. Not a brand. Not a market position. A word — a Sanskrit word that carried the code of the entire enterprise. Everything the studio produced was downstream of that word. Every character, every story, every frame of clay animation was an expression of Prema. The love was not the marketing. The love was the architecture.

And he never sold. This is the part that the industry could not understand and still cannot understand. Art Clokey maintained control of Gumby for his entire life. He died at eighty-eight, on January 8, 2010, still holding the rights, still the owner, still the steward. He had offers. He had pressure. He had decades of an industry telling him that the smart move was to sell — to take the money, let someone else handle the distribution, let the professionals scale the brand.

He refused. Not because he was naive. Because he understood the seed-over-sword principle in his bones. The moment he sold, the sword would take the seed. The industry would acquire the form — the green shape, the name, the brand — and discard the code. Prema would be stripped out because Prema does not optimize. Universal love does not scale the way the market wants things to scale. The soul would be replaced by the strategy, and the thing that was planted would become the thing that was sold.

Art chose the seed. He stayed small. He stayed in control. He stayed faithful to the word that named the studio and the love that fueled the work. And when he died, the seed was still intact — encoded, alive, waiting for the right soil.

The Sword Came Anyway

But the seed does not exist in a vacuum. The sword is always present. The sword is always looking for things to cut.

The entertainment industry is the sword's territory. Not because every person in the industry is malicious — most are not. But because the structure of the industry is the sword's architecture. Consolidation. Gatekeeping. Forced scarcity. The industry does not exist to cultivate stories. The industry exists to control the distribution of stories — and control is the sword's business.

When Art died, the seed was exposed. It was still alive, still carrying the code, still encoded with Prema. But the gardener was gone. The man who had held the sword at bay for sixty years was no longer standing over the garden.

My father picked up the work. Joe Clokey became president of Premavision and spent eight years trying to bring Gumby back — not as a brand, not as intellectual property, but as the living thing Art had planted. He pitched Classic Media, the Jim Henson Company, Amazon, Papercutz. He spent six years with Classic Media alone and nothing was produced. He co-authored Gumby Imagined with my mother, Joan, in November 2017 — a book that documented the art, the vision, the Prema behind the clay.

And then he died. March 2, 2018. Cardiac arrest. He was fifty-six.

The sword did not kill my father with a blade. The sword killed my father with time. With rejection. With the slow grinding pressure of a system that does not reward principle. Eight years of pitching to people who saw a brand and could not see the seed. Eight years of fighting for something the industry had already decided to acquire on its own terms or ignore entirely.

The sword is patient when it needs to be. The sword knows that the gardener will eventually tire, and then the garden is undefended, and then the form can be acquired and the code can be discarded.

But Seeds Outlive Swords

And yet.

Here is what the sword does not understand, has never understood, will never understand:

The seed does not need the gardener to survive. The seed is the information itself. The code is in the structure. The seed can be buried, forgotten, carried by wind to a place no one intended, dropped in soil that no one prepared — and it will grow. Because the seed does not need permission. The seed does not need a strategy. The seed needs soil. And soil is everywhere.

Art planted the seed. The sword took the gardener. Then the sword took the next gardener. And the sword looked at the empty garden and said: now it is mine. Fox acquired Gumby in 2022 — film, TV, streaming, consumer products, everything. The form belongs to the sword now.

But the seed is not the form.

The seed is Prema. The seed is the philosophy encoded in the clay — the green life that walks into books, the imagination that the blocks cannot follow, the love that named the studio before the first frame was shot. The seed is the code, and the code is not in the intellectual property filing. The code is in the story. The code is in the family. The code is in the blood of the man who watched his grandfather create and his father fight and his father die and then stood in the empty garden and heard the word that was planted before he was born.

The sword took the tree. The seed is in the ground.

This is always the pattern. The sword is always faster. The seed is always deeper. The sword controls the present. The seed inherits the future. The sword looks at the cut stump and declares victory. The seed is already germinating in places the sword cannot see.

The Principle Applied

The seed does not argue with the sword. This is critical. This is the part that every activist, every revolutionary, every reformer misses.

The seed does not argue. The seed does not protest. The seed does not file a countersuit or launch a competing campaign or write an angry letter to the editor. The seed does not engage the sword on the sword's terms — because the sword's terms are the sword's territory, and any seed that picks up a sword has become a sword.

The seed waits. The seed grows. The seed outlasts.

Not passively. Not in resignation. Not in the defeated patience of someone who has given up. In the active patience of a living thing that knows its own timetable. The seed grows on its schedule, in its soil, according to its code. The sword cannot accelerate the seed. The sword cannot slow the seed. The sword can cut the visible growth, but the root system is beyond the blade's reach.

This is the principle that governs everything that follows in this book. Everything about the Clokey story — Art's planting, Joe's tending, the storm that took them both, the scattering that followed, and the new growth that is emerging now — follows the seed-over-sword principle.

The seed is quiet. The sword is loud.

The seed is slow. The sword is fast.

The seed is deep. The sword is sharp.

And the seed wins. Not in the battle — the sword wins every battle. But in the war. The long war. The war that is not fought with blades but with roots. The war that is not won in a single season but across generations. The war where the victory is not the destruction of the enemy but the growth of something so alive, so deep, so embedded in the soil of the world that the enemy becomes irrelevant.

The chapters that follow tell the story of three generations who lived by this principle — some knowingly, some not. A grandfather who planted. A father who tended. A storm that scattered. And a son who is learning what grows from the scattered seeds.

The seed over the sword.

The root over the blade.

The code over the form.

The living thing, always, over the dead machine that tried to contain it.

The seed does not argue with the sword. It waits. It grows. It outlasts.

What was planted in love cannot be uprooted by force. What was encoded in clay cannot be acquired by contract. What was spoken into the ground before you were born is speaking still.

Listen. The seed is growing.

The Names That Wrote Themselves: A Living Age Written in a Family's Names

The Discovery

There is a tradition, older than any institution, that names carry meaning — that the word spoken over a life shapes what that life becomes. In Hebrew scripture, God renames Abram to Abraham, Jacob to Israel, Simon to Peter. The name is not a label. It is a commission. A word spoken before the story begins, encoding the story's arc into a single sound.

The Clokey family did not plan this. No one sat down and designed a naming system that would, across generations and marriages and branches, spell out a unified vision for living architecture, voluntary order, and biological design. And yet — when you lay the names out and read what they mean — they say exactly that.

Every name in the family points to the same element: earth. Every name describes the same work: tending living things on a foundation of stone. Every name prophesies the same project — the one now called the Living Age.

The names wrote themselves.

The Foundation: Clokey

The family name comes from Irish Gaelic cloch — stone. Rock. Bedrock. The earth element at its deepest and most immovable.

Stone does not react. Stone does not chase. Stone waits. It holds its ground while everything above it shifts — weather, seasons, empires, industries. The clay on top can be shaped, sold, transferred, rebranded. The stone beneath it never moves.

Art Clokey spent his life shaping clay — soft earth, pliable, transformable. He made a green figure that walked into books and showed children that the world changes through imagination. But his name was never Clay. His name was Stone. The clay was what he worked with. The stone was what he was. The foundation under the creation.

In 2022, the clay was sold. Fox acquired Gumby — the shaped form, the character, the intellectual property. They bought what sat on top of the stone. They did not buy the stone. They cannot. Cloch does not transfer. The bedrock stays with the land it belongs to.

Jesus said it in Matthew 7: "Everyone who hears these words of mine and puts them into practice is like a wise man who built his house on the rock." A sage who built his house on Clokey. It was already written.

The Birth Name: Farrington

Art Clokey was not born a Clokey. He was born Arthur Charles Farrington.

Arthur — from Celtic artos, the bear. But Arthur in legend is not defined by strength. He is defined by return. Rex quondam, rexque futurus — the once and future king. The one who falls at Camlann, is carried to Avalon, and comes back when the land needs him. Arthur is the king whose reign is not continuous but cyclical — he goes away and he returns.

Charles — from Germanic karl, meaning free man. Not a serf, not a subject, not a dependent of any institution. A sovereign individual.

Farrington — from Old English fearn (fern) + tun (enclosure, settlement). A settlement among ferns. A living shelter.

Art Clokey's birth name: the returning king, a free man, in a living shelter.

His birth name described exactly what he would spend his life creating — living things, green things, growing things that shelter the imagination. Gumby was a fern enclosure. A green space where children could enter and be protected by a world that transformed around them. Farrington.

When he was adopted, he received the name Clokey — stone. The birth name described the vision. The adopted name gave it a foundation. The fern enclosure, built on rock.

The Grandmothers

Ruth (Parkander) Clokey

Ruth — Hebrew, meaning companion, friend, woman of loyalty. But the name is the book. In scripture, Ruth is the Moabite woman — the foreigner — who refuses to leave her mother-in-law Naomi after both their husbands die. "Where you go I will go. Your people will be my people and your God my God." She enters the royal lineage of Israel not by blood but by voluntary loyalty — by choosing to stay when the world told her to leave. Ruth becomes the great-grandmother of King David. The line of kings begins with a woman who chose.

Parkander — Swedish. Park (enclosed ground) + -ander (from Greek andros, man). The steward of the kept ground. The one who tends the enclosed living space. Not the owner — the keeper. The one whose role is not dominion but cultivation.

Parkander is the Spiral Steward. The same word, centuries earlier, in a different language. The philosophy Sage would arrive at through bioinformatics, Hayek, and the study of spontaneous order — his grandmother already carried it in her maiden name.

Ruth Parkander Clokey: the loyal companion, steward of the kept ground, on stone. The woman who stays, tends, and holds the foundation.

Joan (Helmstein) Landis

Joan — from Hebrew Yohanan, meaning God is gracious. The feminine form of John — the name of the Baptist, the one at the Jordan, the one who baptizes with water and says someone greater is coming.

Helmstein — German. Helm (protection, shelter) + Stein (stone). Protected stone. The stone that shelters. The -stein suffix is one of the strongest markers of Ashkenazi Jewish heritage in German surnames — families who carried the earth in their names across centuries of European diaspora.

Landis — German. Land — earth, ground, territory. The land itself. Another surname common in Ashkenazi Jewish families from German-speaking regions.

Joan Helmstein Landis: God's grace, the protected stone, of the land. Grace held in stone, rooted in the earth.

What the Grandmothers Carry

| Grandmother | Name | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Ruth (Parkander) Clokey | Loyal companion, steward of the ground, on stone | The choice that begins the royal line | | Joan (Helmstein) Landis | God's grace, protected stone, of the land | The grace that shelters the foundation |

One carries loyalty — the voluntary bond that creates lineage. The other carries grace — the divine protection that preserves it. Between them, the two grandmothers hold the foundation from both sides: Ruth tends the stone. Joan shelters it.

The Josephs

The name Joseph appears across the family like a thread woven through every branch.

Joseph — Hebrew, Yosef, meaning "he will add" or "God will increase." In Genesis, Joseph is the son who is sold by his brothers into a foreign land, stripped of everything, descending into prison — and through that descent, he rises to feed the nations and save the family that rejected him. The pattern: betrayal, exile, descent, and then — God increases through him.

Joseph Parkander — God will increase through the steward of the ground.

Joseph Walden Clokey — Walden from Old English weald, meaning forest, woodland, the wild living place. Thoreau went to Walden to find truth by leaving the institution and entering the living world. God will increase through the forest on stone.

Joseph Arthur Clokey — God will increase through the returning king on stone.

Three Josephs. Three branches. Three times the same promise: what is cast down will rise. What is sold into foreign hands will become the means of salvation. What descends will increase.

Gumby was sold. The family lost the clay. And through that loss — through the descent — the Living Age grows. New clay on the same stone. God increases.

The Children of Stone

Art and Ruth Clokey's grandchildren carry the pattern into the third generation:

Shasta Joseph Clokey

Shasta — Mount Shasta, the sacred volcanic peak of northern California. A dormant volcano — fire held inside stone. The mountain that looks still on the surface but carries molten energy within. Sacred to the native peoples of the region as a place where the spirit world meets the physical.

A volcano is the earth element containing fire. Stone holding flame. Dormant does not mean dead. It means waiting.

Joseph — God will increase.

Clokey — Stone.

The sacred mountain with fire inside, through whom God will increase, on stone. Fire waiting within the earth for the moment of release.

Sequoia Jo Ana Rose Clokey

Sequoia — the largest living things on earth. Giant redwoods that grow for thousands of years, that survive fire because their bark is fire-resistant. The forest that endures everything. And the name comes from Sequoyah, the Cherokee scholar who gave his people a written language — a single man who didn't conquer or rule but gave his people the power of the word.

Jo — Joseph. God will increase.

Ana — from Hebrew Hannah, meaning grace, favor.

Rose — the flower that grows from thorns. In Judges 9, Jotham warns that only the thornbush accepts the crown. But the rose grows from the thorn. The living thing that transforms what is sharp into what is beautiful.

Clokey — Stone.

The living thing that endures fire, through whom God's grace increases, the flower from thorns, on stone. The tree that stands for millennia. The word made visible. The thorn redeemed.

Sage Arthur Jordan Clokey

Sage — from Latin sapere, to taste, to perceive, to know directly. Not book knowledge — the wisdom that recognizes truth on contact, the way a tongue recognizes sweetness. The serpent's gift — seeing clearly.

Arthur — the returning king. The third Arthur in the Clokey line. Rex quondam, rexque futurus.

Jordan — from Hebrew Yarden, meaning the descender. The river that flows down — from Mount Hermon to the Dead Sea, the lowest point on earth. The Jordan is where Jesus was baptized. The dove descended there. The river that goes to the lowest place so that what passes through it can rise.

Clokey — Stone.

The one who sees clearly, the king who returns, descending to the lowest place, on stone. The sage who goes down before he goes up, built on what cannot be moved.

The Three Together

| Child | Name meaning | Element | |---|---|---| | Shasta Joseph Clokey | Mountain with fire inside — waiting on stone | Earth holding fire | | Sequoia Jo Ana Rose Clokey | Tree that survives fire — grace from thorns on stone | Earth enduring fire | | Sage Arthur Jordan Clokey | The one who sees — returning king — descending — on stone | Earth, water, wisdom |

A mountain. A tree. A sage. Three children named for things that endure — the peak that holds fire, the tree that outlasts it, and the wisdom that sees through it. All rooted in stone. All carrying Joseph — the promise that God will increase through what was cast down.

The Pattern

Lay the names out as a single text and read them in sequence:

| Name | Meaning | |---|---| | Farrington | The living shelter — settlement among ferns | | Clokey | Stone — the bedrock | | Parkander | Steward of the kept ground — the Spiral Steward | | Ruth | The loyal companion who enters the royal line by choice | | Helmstein | Protected stone — the stone that shelters | | Landis | Land — the earth itself | | Joseph | God will increase — what is cast down will rise | | Walden | The forest — the wild living place | | Arthur | The returning king | | Charles | Free man | | Shasta | The mountain with fire inside | | Sequoia | The tree that endures — the word made visible | | Sage | The one who sees | | Jordan | The descender — the river, the baptism | | Rose | The flower from thorns |

Every name is the earth element. Every name describes either the foundation (stone, rock, land), the living thing that grows from it (fern, forest, sequoia, rose), or the one who tends it (steward, farmer, sage). No name in the family points to empire, conquest, industry, or domination. No name means king in the worldly sense — even Arthur is the king who returns, who serves the round table, who falls and comes back. These are names of ground, growth, endurance, and stewardship.

The family was named for the Living Age before the Living Age had a name.

What the Names Say

Read together, the Clokey family names are a single statement:

Build a living shelter on stone. Tend the ground. Stay loyal to the foundation. Let God increase through what is cast down. Carry the fire inside the mountain. Stand like the tree that survives the burning. See clearly. Descend before you rise. And when the time comes — return.

This is the Living Age.

Not a business plan. Not a startup. Not a technology company. A commission written in names before anyone alive could read it.

Farrington — the living shelter. That is what living architecture builds. Parkander — the steward of the ground. That is what the Spiral Steward does. Clokey — stone. That is what the foundation is. Joseph — God will increase. That is the promise. Arthur — the king returns. That is the arc. Sage — the one who sees. That is who reads it. Jordan — the descender. That is the path.

The names wrote themselves. The Living Age is just learning to read them.

Sage Arthur Jordan Clokey The Living Age April 2026

Chapter 16: Art and the Green Knight

The Orphan

He was born Arthur Charles Farrington on October 12, 1921, in Detroit, Michigan. His father left before he could remember. His mother could not keep him. By the age of nine, he was an orphan.

I have thought about this more than almost anything else in the Clokey story — not the fame, not the legacy, not the green clay figure that became an American icon, but the orphaning. Because everything that followed grew from this wound. Every act of creation Art Clokey ever performed was, in some way, a response to having been abandoned by the people who were supposed to hold him. Everything he built was shelter. Everything he imagined was a world where a small, flexible, green figure could walk into any story and belong.

An orphan at nine. Placed in foster care. Moved through the system the way the system moves children — as a problem to be managed, not a soul to be cultivated. And then, a rescue. Joseph Waddell Clokey — a composer, a man of music and gentleness — adopted the boy. Gave him a name. Gave him a home. Gave him the thing the system could not provide: a father.

Art Clokey was made by two forces. The first was abandonment — the knowledge, burned into his childhood, that the world does not owe you belonging. That the people who are supposed to stay can leave. That the structure you depend on can vanish. This knowledge does one of two things to a person: it makes you desperate to belong to any structure that will have you, or it makes you determined to build a structure so real, so true, so rooted in something deeper than convenience, that it cannot abandon you — because you are the one who made it.

Art became the second kind.

The other force was Joseph Clokey — the adoptive father, the composer, the man who showed Art that creation is not commerce. That music exists because beauty exists. That a man can spend his life making something that does not sell, and the making is still worth the life. Joseph did not teach Art to be an artist. Joseph showed Art what an artist looked like — and Art spent the rest of his life becoming one.

The Education

He studied at Hartford Art School and Pomona College before arriving at the University of Southern California for film school. There, he found the teacher who gave him the language for what he had always felt.

Slavko Vorkapich was a Serbian filmmaker and montage theorist who understood cinema not as a commercial medium but as a language — a language of movement, rhythm, and transformation. Vorkapich taught that film was not about recording reality. Film was about revealing reality — showing the dynamics beneath the surface, the forces that move through matter, the aliveness of the physical world.

Under Vorkapich, Art made Gumbasia in 1953. A short film. Abstract clay shapes moving to jazz — stretching, compressing, transforming, alive with rhythm. No characters. No story. Just clay being alive. Moving the way music moves. Changing the way living things change. Becoming and unbecoming and becoming again.

Gumbasia was a thesis film. It was also a Genesis. The entire Gumby universe — the clay, the transformation, the idea that form is not fixed, that the living thing can change and grow and enter any story — was encoded in those few minutes of abstract movement. The seed was planted in a university film lab, and it carried inside it the code for sixty years of creation.

From Gumbasia came the idea of a character. A figure made of the same transformable clay. Green — the color of growth, of chlorophyll, of every living thing that converts light into life. Soft, pliable, capable of stretching into any shape without breaking. A figure that could walk into books — literally enter the stories — and become part of whatever world it found there.

Gumby was born. And with Gumby, Art Clokey found the form that would carry his philosophy for the rest of his life.

The Philosophy

Art Clokey was a man on a spiritual search that never ended.

He started in the Episcopal church — the adopted boy seeking structure, seeking the father, seeking the institution that would not leave. He studied at Hartford Theological Seminary. But the institution could not hold him, because Art was not looking for an institution. He was looking for God — and God, as Art would discover over and over throughout his life, does not live in institutions.

In the 1960s, the counterculture opened doors that the seminary had kept closed. Art explored Buddhism. He sat in meditation. He studied Eastern philosophy — not as an academic exercise but as a man genuinely trying to understand the structure of consciousness, the nature of compassion, the relationship between the inner life and the outer form.

And then he found Sathya Sai Baba — the Indian guru who taught universal love as the foundation of all spiritual practice. Prema. The word that would name the studio. The word that would fund every creative decision Art ever made. The word that was the root system beneath the clay.

I want to be careful here, because the tendency of the modern mind is to flatten this journey into a caricature — to call Art a "spiritual seeker" the way the culture calls people "spiritual but not religious," as though the seeking were a phase rather than a discipline. Art's spiritual journey was not dilettantism. It was rigorous. It was costly. It was the organizing principle of his creative life.

He studied at an Episcopal seminary and learned the rigor of systematic theology. He practiced Buddhist meditation and learned the discipline of inner silence. He followed Sai Baba and learned the power of universal love as a structural principle. And he poured all of it — every tradition, every practice, every hard-won insight — into the clay.

Davey and Goliath was explicitly Christian — a clay-animated series produced for the Lutheran Church in America, telling stories of faith, conscience, and moral courage. Gumby was implicitly spiritual — a green figure who could enter any story, who was soft enough to change but strong enough to return to his own shape, whose enemies were rigid and geometric and incapable of imagination.

The man was not confused. The man was comprehensive. He drew from every well because he was building something that was meant to hold water from all of them. Premavision was not a denomination. It was a wellspring — and the water came from everywhere that water comes from, because water does not respect the boundaries that institutions draw around their sources.

The Green Knight

In the medieval poem Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, a massive green figure rides into King Arthur's court on New Year's Day and issues a challenge. He offers any knight the chance to strike him with his own axe — on the condition that the knight must find him one year later and receive the same blow.

Sir Gawain accepts. He cuts off the Green Knight's head. The Green Knight picks up his head, reminds Gawain of the bargain, and rides away.

The Green Knight is a test. He is not an enemy in the conventional sense — he does not seek to conquer Camelot or overthrow Arthur. He seeks to test the integrity of the court. He rides in with an impossible challenge and watches to see who has the courage to accept it and the honor to fulfill it. The entire poem is about one question: will the knight keep his word, even when it costs him everything?

Art Clokey was the Green Knight of children's entertainment.

He rode into the industry with something impossible — a green clay figure in a world of hand-drawn animation. A character built on spiritual principles in an industry built on commercial ones. A studio named after a Sanskrit word for love in a marketplace that spoke only the language of profit.

And he issued a challenge. Not in words — in being. The challenge was: can you deal with someone who will not sell? Can your industry accommodate a creator who maintains full ownership, full control, full alignment between the vision and the product? Can you work with a man whose bottom line is not money but meaning?

The industry could not. Not because Art was difficult — by all accounts, he was generous, warm, eccentric in the way that genuinely creative people are eccentric. But because the industry's architecture is the sword's architecture. Consolidation. Acquisition. The conversion of art into intellectual property and intellectual property into revenue. The industry does not know what to do with a creator who will not convert. The industry's entire machinery runs on the assumption that everything is for sale — and when it encounters something that is not, the machinery grinds and stalls and eventually moves on to the next acquisition.

Art held. For sixty years, he held. Through the original Gumby series in the 1950s. Through the lean years. Through the revival in the 1980s — The Gumby Show returned, and Art directed new episodes in his sixties, still making clay move, still telling stories about a green figure who could enter any book. Through the merchandising boom when Gumby became a pop culture icon and the offers to sell must have been staggering. Through the quiet years at the end, when the culture had moved on to digital animation and clay was considered quaint.

He held.

He held the way a root holds the soil — not by grasping, but by going deeper. He did not fight the industry. He did not protest the system. He did not write manifestos or launch campaigns or make himself a symbol of resistance. He simply continued to create, on his own terms, from his own studio, funded by his own vision, aligned with the word he had chosen: Prema.

The Green Knight does not fight the court. The Green Knight tests the court. And if the court fails the test, the Green Knight does not burn Camelot to the ground. He simply reveals — by his presence, by his persistence, by his refusal to play by the court's rules — that the court's integrity was always thinner than it claimed.

Art Clokey revealed, by his life, that the entertainment industry has no integrity. Not because every person in it is corrupt, but because the structure does not reward integrity. The structure rewards sale. The structure rewards conversion. The structure rewards the transformation of the living thing into the dead product. And a man who refuses to convert — who holds the living thing alive for sixty years, who dies at eighty-eight still owning what he created, still aligned with the word he spoke at the beginning — that man is a standing rebuke to the entire architecture.

January 8, 2010

Art Clokey died on January 8, 2010, at his home in Clovis, California. He was eighty-eight years old.

He died owning Gumby. He died owning Premavision. He died with the rights intact, the vision uncompromised, the word still spoken. Sixty years of creation, and not one frame sold to someone who did not understand what it meant.

I was five years old. I do not remember his death the way my father remembered it — I was too young to understand what had been lost. But I understand now. I understand that what died on January 8, 2010, was not just a man. It was a proof of concept. It was the living evidence that a human being could hold something pure for an entire lifetime without selling it. That the seed-over-sword principle was not just philosophy — it was possible. One man had done it. One man had proved it could be done.

The orphan boy who was abandoned at nine built a world of imagination, named it after universal love, held it for six decades, and left it to his family with the soul still intact. He did not just create a character. He proved that a man could hold something pure his entire life without selling it.

The Green Knight tested the industry and the industry failed. But the Green Knight was never defeated. He rode back out the way he rode in — green, alive, impossible — and the court was left to reckon with what it had been shown.

Art Clokey was the green figure in a world of blocks. Soft where the industry was hard. Alive where the industry was mechanical. Transformable where the industry was rigid. He walked into the story of American entertainment and became part of it without becoming of it. He entered the book without losing himself in the book.

And when he left, the book was different. The story had been changed. Not by force — by presence. By sixty years of being the green thing in a yellow world. By proving that the seed outlasts the sword, that love outlasts commerce, that the man who will not sell is the man the industry cannot forget.

Art Clokey. Born Arthur Charles Farrington. Orphaned at nine. Adopted by a composer. Educated by a visionary. Transformed by love. Green Knight of children's entertainment.

He held the seed for sixty years and never let the sword touch it.

Premavision.

The vision of universal love.

The root that holds.

Chapter 17: Joe and the Storm

My Father

I need to tell you about my father.

Not the facts first — though the facts matter and I will give them to you. Not the timeline, the résumé, the list of accomplishments that people read at memorials and nod at without feeling. I need to tell you about the man. The actual human being who raised me. The one who drove me to school and picked me up from practice and sat at the kitchen table explaining things I did not yet have the capacity to understand. The one who believed — with a conviction I have only begun to appreciate in the years since he left — that the world could be made better by one man holding the line.
